34.notes:
Job - Chapter 34
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Elihu's second speech — defends God's justice, arguing it is unthinkable that the Almighty would pervert justice since He governs all nations impartially and sees all human deeds without need of investigation.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown (see Chapter 1 notes for full discussion).

Historical Context: Chapter 34 is Elihu's second speech, and
here he turns to defend God's justice directly. Elihu addresses
"wise men" and "men of understanding" — possibly the three
friends or a broader audience. His argument centers on one
assertion: "Far be it from God, that he should do wickedness;
and from the Almighty, that he should commit iniquity" (v.10).
God repays according to works (v.11), does not pervert justice
(v.12), sees all (v.21), and is no respecter of persons (v.19).
Elihu's defense of God's justice is stronger than the three
friends' because he does not ground it in observable retribution
but in God's nature itself. God is just because God IS just —
not because we can always see justice operating.

Elihu Quotes Job (vv.5-9): Elihu paraphrases Job's claims: "I am
righteous: and God hath taken away my judgment" (v.5); "It
profiteth a man nothing that he should delight himself with God"
(v.9). These are fair, if reductive, summaries of Job's
positions. Elihu treats these as dangerous conclusions that must
be refuted.

God's Justice Defended (vv.10-15): God repays each according to
his ways (v.11). He cannot do wickedly (v.12). No one appointed
Him over the earth — He is self-authorized (v.13). If He withdrew
His breath, all flesh would perish (vv.14-15). God's justice is
inherent, not delegated. He answers to no one because there is
no one above Him.

God's Sovereignty Over All (vv.16-30): God governs nations and
individuals alike (v.29). He shows no partiality to princes or
the rich (v.19) because "they all are the work of his hands"
(v.19). He sees all — no darkness can hide the wicked (v.22). He
judges the mighty "without number" (v.24). His purpose: "That
the hypocrite reign not, lest the people be ensnared" (v.30).

Elihu's Harsh Conclusion (vv.34-37): Elihu concludes harshly:
Job "speaks without knowledge" (v.35), should be "tried unto the
end" (v.36), and "adds rebellion unto his sin" (v.37). This goes
too far — Elihu moves from defending God to attacking Job. His
desire that Job be tested further seems cruel and exceeds his
earlier empathy.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.10             | Deuteronomy | 32      | 4                  | "The Rock, his work is perfect, for all his ways are justice. A God of faithfulness and without iniquity." (ESV)
v.11             | Romans      | 2       | 6                  | "He will render to each one according to his works." (ESV)
v.12             | Genesis     | 18      | 25                 | "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?" (ESV) Abraham's similar appeal to God's justice
v.14             | Psalm       | 104     | 29                 | "When you take away their breath, they die and return to their dust." (ESV)
v.19             | Acts        | 10      | 34                 | "God shows no partiality." (ESV)
v.19             | Romans      | 2       | 11                 | "For God shows no partiality." (ESV)
v.21             | Proverbs    | 15      | 3                  | "The eyes of the LORD are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good." (ESV)
v.22             | Psalm       | 139     | 12                 | "The darkness is not dark to you; the night is bright as the day." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-34/
  Guzik notes that Elihu's defense of God's justice (vv.10-12)
  is theologically sound and avoids the simplistic retribution
  logic of the three friends. However, his conclusion (vv.34-37)
  goes too far — wanting Job "tried unto the end" reveals a
  punitive streak that undermines his earlier compassion. Guzik
  observes that v.9 is a misquotation of Job: Job never said it
  profits nothing to delight in God — he said his righteous
  conduct had not been rewarded. The distinction matters.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Elihu stands on solid ground when he declares God cannot do
  wickedness. This is not a hope — it is a certainty grounded in
  God's nature. Unlike the friends, Elihu does not argue backward
  from Job's suffering to Job's sin. He argues forward from God's
  character to God's trustworthiness. The logic is right: if God
  IS just, then even when we CANNOT SEE justice operating, we can
  trust it exists. But Elihu falls when he becomes prosecutor
  rather than theologian — when he desires Job's further testing
  rather than Job's restoration."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.10             | KJV         | "far be it from God" / ESV: "far be it from God" — Hebrew "chalilah" — the same strong rejection used by Job in 27:5 ("God forbid"). Elihu uses Job's own language to make the opposite point.
v.14             | KJV         | "gather unto himself his spirit and his breath" / ESV: "gather to himself his spirit and his breath" — If God withdrew the life-breath He loaned to creation, all would die instantly. Creation's existence depends moment by moment on God's sustaining will.
v.36             | KJV         | "tried unto the end" / ESV: "tried to the end" — Hebrew "yibbachen" = tested/examined. Elihu wants Job's testing prolonged — a harsh wish that seems to lack compassion. This is arguably Elihu's worst moment.
v.37             | KJV         | "clappeth his hands among us" / ESV: "claps his hands among us" — A gesture of mockery or defiance. Elihu accuses Job of adding arrogant rebellion to whatever sin originally caused his suffering.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Respect of        | Hebrew "nasa panim" = to lift the face (of another),                              | 
persons           | meaning to show favoritism based on status or appearance. God does NOT do this — He judges all equally regardless of wealth, power, or position. | 
Without hand      | v.20: "the mighty shall be taken away without hand" — removed by God's direct action, not human agency. God's judgment needs no human instrument (cf. Daniel 2:34, the stone "cut without hands"). | KJV term
Rebellion         | Hebrew "pesha" = revolt, transgression, breach of trust. Elihu's accusation in v.37 is that Job's continued protest constitutes rebellion against God — a charge stronger than the friends made. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's justice is grounded in His nature, not in our observation (v.10).
   We cannot always SEE justice operating, but we can trust it EXISTS
   because God cannot be unjust. Faith trusts God's character when God's
   actions are unclear.

2. All humanity is equally God's creation (v.19). Rich and poor, powerful
   and weak — "they all are the work of his hands." This is the ultimate
   basis for human equality and impartial justice. If God shows no
   favoritism, neither should His people.

3. God sustains existence moment by moment (vv.14-15). Every breath is
   borrowed. If God withdrew His sustaining power for one instant, all life
   would cease. Gratitude, not complaint, should be our default posture — we
   exist by God's continuous choice.

4. Defending God can become attacking people (vv.36-37). Elihu starts by
   defending God's justice (good) and ends by wishing prolonged suffering on
   Job (bad). Zeal for God's honor can become cruelty toward God's people if
   it loses compassion. Defend truth without destroying people.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Job Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/job/

David Guzik — Job 34 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-34/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

35.ESV:
1. And Elihu answered and said:
2. “Do you think this to be just? Do you say, ‘It is my right before God,’
3. that you ask, ‘What advantage have I? How am I better off than if I had sinned?’
4. I will answer you and your friends with you.
5. Look at the heavens, and see; and behold the clouds, which are higher than you.
6. If you have sinned, what do you accomplish against him? And if your transgressions are multiplied, what do you do to him?
7. If you are righteous, what do you give to him? Or what does he receive from your hand?
8. Your wickedness concerns a man like yourself, and your righteousness a son of man.
9. “Because of the multitude of oppressions people cry out; they call for help because of the arm of the mighty.
10. But none says, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night,
11. who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the heavens?’
12. There they cry out, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil men.
13. Surely God does not hear an empty cry, nor does the Almighty regard it.
14. How much less when you say that you do not see him, that the case is before him, and you are waiting for him!
15. And now, because his anger does not punish, and he does not take much note of transgression,
16. Job opens his mouth in empty talk; he multiplies words without knowledge.”
35.KJV:
1. Elihu spake moreover, and said,
2. Thinkest thou this to be right, that thou saidst, My righteousness is more than God’s?
3. For thou saidst, What advantage will it be unto thee? and, What profit shall I have, if I be cleansed from my sin?
4. I will answer thee, and thy companions with thee.
5. Look unto the heavens, and see; and behold the clouds which are higher than thou.
6. If thou sinnest, what doest thou against him? or if thy transgressions be multiplied, what doest thou unto him?
7. If thou be righteous, what givest thou him? or what receiveth he of thine hand?
8. Thy wickedness may hurt a man as thou art; and thy righteousness may profit the son of man.
9. By reason of the multitude of oppressions they make the oppressed to cry: they cry out by reason of the arm of the mighty.
10. But none saith, Where is God my maker, who giveth songs in the night;
11. Who teacheth us more than the beasts of the earth, and maketh us wiser than the fowls of heaven?
12. There they cry, but none giveth answer, because of the pride of evil men.
13. Surely God will not hear vanity, neither will the Almighty regard it.
14. Although thou sayest thou shalt not see him, yet judgment is before him; therefore trust thou in him.
15. But now, because it is not so, he hath visited in his anger; yet he knoweth it not in great extremity:
16. Therefore doth Job open his mouth in vain; he multiplieth words without knowledge.
35.ASV:
1. Moreover Elihu answered and said,
2. Thinkest thou this to be [thy] right, [Or] sayest thou, My righteousness is more than God`s,
3. That thou sayest, What advantage will it be unto thee? [And], What profit shall I have, more than if I had sinned?
4. I will answer thee, And thy companions with thee.
5. Look unto the heavens, and see; And behold the skies, which are higher than thou.
6. If thou hast sinned, what effectest thou against him? And if thy transgressions be multiplied, what doest thou unto him?
7. If thou be righteous, what givest thou him? Or what receiveth he of thy hand?
8. Thy wickedness [may hurt] a man as thou art; And thy righteousness [may profit] a son of man.
9. By reason of the multitude of oppressions they cry out; They cry for help by reason of the arm of the mighty.
10. But none saith, Where is God my Maker, Who giveth songs in the night,
11. Who teacheth us more than the beasts of the earth, And maketh us wiser than the birds of the heavens?
12. There they cry, but none giveth answer, Because of the pride of evil men.
13. Surely God will not hear an empty [cry], Neither will the Almighty regard it.
14. How much less when thou sayest thou beholdest him not, The cause is before him, and thou waitest for him!
15. But now, because he hath not visited in his anger, Neither doth he greatly regard arrogance;
16. Therefore doth Job open his mouth in vanity; He multiplieth words without knowledge.
35.NET:
1. Then Elihu answered:
2. “Do you think this to be just when you say, ‘My right before God’?
3. But you say, ‘What will it profit you,’ and, ‘What do I gain by not sinning?’
4. I will reply to you, and to your friends with you.
5. Gaze at the heavens and see; consider the clouds, which are higher than you.
6. If you sin, how does it affect God? If your transgressions are many, what does it do to him?
7. If you are righteous, what do you give to God, or what does he receive from your hand?
8. Your wickedness affects only a person like yourself, and your righteousness only other people.
9. “People cry out because of the excess of oppression; they cry out for help because of the power of the mighty.
10. But no one says, ‘Where is God, my Creator, who gives songs in the night,
11. who teaches us more than the wild animals of the earth, and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky?’
12. Then they cry out—but he does not answer— because of the arrogance of the wicked.
13. Surely it is an empty cry—God does not hear it; the Almighty does not take notice of it.
14. How much less, then, when you say that you do not perceive him, that the case is before him and you are waiting for him!
15. And further, when you say that his anger does not punish, and that he does not know transgression!
16. So Job opens his mouth to no purpose; without knowledge he multiplies words.”
35.WEB:
1. Moreover Elihu answered,
2. “Do you think this to be your right, or do you say, ‘My righteousness is more than God’s,’
3. That you ask, ‘What advantage will it be to you? What profit shall I have, more than if I had sinned?’
4. I will answer you, and your companions with you.
5. Look to the heavens, and see. See the skies, which are higher than you.
6. If you have sinned, what effect do you have against him? If your transgressions are multiplied, what do you do to him?
7. If you are righteous, what do you give him? Or what does he receive from your hand?
8. Your wickedness may hurt a man as you are, and your righteousness may profit a son of man.
9. “By reason of the multitude of oppressions they cry out. They cry for help by reason of the arm of the mighty.
10. But no one says, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night,
11. who teaches us more than the animals of the earth, and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky?’
12. There they cry, but no one gives answer, because of the pride of evil men.
13. Surely God will not hear an empty cry, neither will the Almighty regard it.
14. How much less when you say you don’t see him. The cause is before him, and you wait for him!
15. But now, because he has not visited in his anger, neither does he greatly regard arrogance.
16. Therefore Job opens his mouth with empty talk, and he multiplies words without knowledge.”
35.notes:
Job - Chapter 35
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Elihu's third speech — argues that human sin or righteousness does not affect God's essential nature, and that those who cry out in suffering without truly seeking God should not expect an answer.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown (see Chapter 1 notes for full discussion).

Historical Context: Chapter 35 is Elihu's third speech and the
shortest of his four addresses. Here Elihu tackles a question
he attributes to Job: "What advantage have I? How am I better
off than if I had sinned?" (v.3, ESV). Elihu's answer has two
parts: (1) human sin or righteousness does not affect GOD — it
affects other humans (vv.5-8); (2) people who cry out in
suffering but do not genuinely seek God should not expect an
answer (vv.9-16). The first point is theologically significant:
God is not diminished by sin or enriched by righteousness. The
second point is partially valid but harshly applied to Job, who
has been seeking God with all his being (23:3).

God's Self-Sufficiency (vv.5-8): "Look unto the heavens, and see;
and behold the clouds which are higher than thou" (v.5). If you
sin, what do you do TO God? (v.6). If you are righteous, what do
you GIVE God? (v.7). Your wickedness affects men like yourself;
your righteousness benefits fellow humans (v.8). Elihu's point:
God is not personally injured by sin or personally profited by
righteousness. He transcends the transaction. This echoes
Eliphaz's point in 22:2-3, but Elihu draws a different
conclusion: your conduct matters for HUMAN community, not for
divine need.

Why God Does Not Answer Cries (vv.9-13): People cry out under
oppression (v.9) but "none saith, Where is God my maker, who
giveth songs in the night?" (v.10). They cry from pain but not
toward God. They want relief but not relationship. Therefore
"God will not hear vanity, neither will the Almighty regard it"
(v.13). Elihu distinguishes between crying out in pain (which
anyone does) and genuinely seeking God (which requires
intentionality).

Application to Job (vv.14-16): Elihu tells Job: "Although thou
sayest thou shalt not see him, yet judgment is before him;
therefore trust thou in him" (v.14). Job should trust even when
he cannot see. But then Elihu concludes harshly: "Job opens his
mouth in empty talk; he multiplies words without knowledge" (v.16,
ESV). This judgment anticipates God's own rebuke in 38:2 — but
coming from Elihu rather than God, it carries less authority.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.6              | Psalm       | 51      | 4                  | "Against you, you only, have I sinned" (ESV) — while sin affects God relationally, it does not diminish Him essentially
v.7              | Romans      | 11      | 35                 | "Or who has given a gift to him that he might be repaid?" (ESV) — No one places God in debt through righteousness
v.10             | Psalm       | 42      | 8                  | "At night his song is with me" (ESV) — God gives songs even in darkness
v.10             | Acts        | 16      | 25                 | Paul and Silas singing at midnight in prison — an example of receiving God's "songs in the night."
v.11             | Genesis     | 1       | 26-28              | God teaches man more than animals — humans have unique capacity for divine relationship
v.13             | Proverbs    | 15      | 29                 | "The LORD is far from the wicked, but he hears the prayer of the righteous." (ESV)
v.14             | Hebrews     | 11      | 1                  | "Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen." (ESV)
v.14             | 2 Corinthians | 5       | 7                  | "We walk by faith, not by sight."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-35/
  Guzik observes that Elihu's point about God's self-sufficiency
  (vv.5-8) is theologically correct and echoes later Reformed
  theology: God is not enriched by our worship or impoverished by
  our sin. He commands righteousness for our good, not His need.
  However, Guzik notes that the application to Job is unfair: Job
  HAS been seeking God genuinely (23:3), not merely crying in pain.
  Elihu's critique of empty cries applies to others, not to Job.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "God who 'giveth songs in the night' — this is one of the
  tenderest descriptions of God in all Scripture. In the daylight,
  anyone can sing. But in the night — in suffering, darkness,
  confusion — only God can give the song. The melody comes not
  from circumstances but from the Composer Himself. Elihu is
  right that many cry in pain without seeking the Singer. They
  want relief but not the Reliever. They want comfort but not
  the Comforter. God responds to those who seek HIM, not merely
  those who seek escape."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "My righteousness is more than God's?" / ESV: "It is my right before God" — The Hebrew is ambiguous. KJV reads Elihu accusing Job of claiming to be more righteous than God. ESV reads Job claiming his right/case before God. The ESV is more likely: Job claims his case is just, not that he exceeds God in righteousness.
v.10             | KJV         | "Who giveth songs in the night" / ESV: "who gives songs in the night" — Hebrew "zemirot" = songs, psalms. God as the giver of songs in darkness — a metaphor for comfort, hope, and worship even in suffering.
v.15             | KJV         | "he hath visited in his anger" / ESV: "his anger does not punish" — This verse is extremely difficult in Hebrew. The two translations offer nearly opposite readings. The ESV suggests God has been patient (not punishing), and therefore Job presumes too much. The KJV suggests God has visited in anger but Job doesn't recognize it.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Songs in the      | A metaphor for God-given worship, comfort, and hope                               | 
night             | that sustains believers during dark seasons. Night = suffering, confusion, spiritual darkness. Songs = the worship that only God's Spirit can produce in such circumstances (cf. Psalm 42:8; Acts 16:25). | 
Self-             | The theological doctrine (reflected in vv.6-7) that                               | 
sufficiency       | God is complete in Himself, needing nothing from                                  | 
of God            | creation. He is not enriched by worship or diminished by sin. His commands serve human flourishing, not divine need (cf. Acts 17:25). | 
Vanity            | Hebrew "shav" = emptiness, worthlessness. In v.13, "God will not hear vanity" means God does not respond to empty, self-centered cries that seek relief without seeking God Himself. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God does not need your righteousness — but He commands it for your good
   (vv.6-8). Worship is not payment to God; it is participation in what God
   designed for human flourishing. This liberates worship from being
   transactional: you don't serve God to earn; you serve God because it is
   what you were made for.

2. Crying from pain is not the same as seeking God (vv.9-10). Everyone cries
   when they hurt. But not everyone asks "Where is God my Maker?" The
   difference between pain-driven crying and faith-driven seeking determines
   whether we receive God's response. Seek the Person, not just the relief.

3. God gives songs in the night (v.10). This is one of the most beautiful
   promises in Scripture. Darkness does not silence God's music — it is
   precisely where He composes it. If you are in the night, listen for the
   song He is giving.

4. Trust when you cannot see (v.14). Elihu's counsel here is genuinely wise:
   when evidence fails, trust must operate. Faith is not seeing and
   believing — it is NOT seeing and STILL believing. This is precisely what
   Job will ultimately demonstrate.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Job Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/job/

David Guzik — Job 35 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-35/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

36.notes:
Job - Chapter 36
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Elihu's fourth speech begins — proclaims God's greatness in using suffering to open ears and deliver the righteous, then launches into a magnificent description of God's power in storms and rain.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown (see Chapter 1 notes for full discussion).

Historical Context: Chapters 36-37 form Elihu's fourth and final
speech — his longest and most developed argument. Chapter 36
contains Elihu's most important theological contribution: the
concept of suffering as divine discipline that aims at
restoration, not destruction. Unlike the three friends who saw
suffering as punishment for PAST sin, Elihu sees it as prevention
of FUTURE sin and training for greater maturity. The chapter
transitions from theological argument (vv.1-21) into a
magnificent nature hymn describing God's power in storms
(vv.22-33), which will continue into chapter 37 and prepare for
God's own appearance from the whirlwind (38:1).

Elihu Speaks for God (vv.1-4): Elihu claims to "ascribe
righteousness to my Maker" (v.3) and to possess perfect knowledge
(v.4). These claims are bold — some would say presumptuous. Yet
his theological content here is genuinely insightful, even if his
self-assessment is inflated.

God's Character (vv.5-7): "God is mighty, and despiseth not any"
(v.5). God does not preserve the wicked (v.6) but gives right to
the poor. "He withdraweth not his eyes from the righteous" (v.7)
— God watches over the righteous continuously, establishing them.
This directly challenges Job's complaint of divine inattention.

Suffering as Discipline (vv.8-15): THIS is Elihu's most original
insight. If the righteous are "bound in fetters" and "holden in
cords of affliction" (v.8), God uses this to SHOW them their
transgressions (v.9), OPEN their ears to instruction (v.10), and
COMMAND their return from iniquity (v.10). If they obey, they
prosper (v.11). If they refuse, they perish (v.12). "He
delivereth the poor in his affliction, and openeth their ears
in oppression" (v.15). Suffering is not God's anger but God's
classroom. Pain is not retribution but education.

Application to Job (vv.16-21): God was alluring Job "out of
distress into a broad place" (v.16), but Job has been filled
with "the judgment of the wicked" (v.17) — complaining rather
than learning. Elihu warns: do not desire the night (v.20), do
not turn to iniquity rather than accepting affliction (v.21).

God's Power in Nature (vv.22-33): The chapter transitions into
nature poetry: God is exalted in power (v.22), teaches uniquely
(v.22), cannot be told His way (v.23), is great and unsearchable
(v.26). He governs the water cycle — drawing up drops, distilling
rain from clouds (vv.27-28). His thunder and lightning declare
His presence (vv.29-33). This nature imagery bridges directly
into chapter 37 and prepares for God's theophany in chapter 38.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.5              | 2 Peter     | 3       | 9                  | "Not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance." (ESV) God despises none
v.7              | Psalm       | 34      | 15                 | "The eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry." (ESV)
v.8              | Psalm       | 107     | 10-14              | Those bound in iron and affliction whom God delivers when they cry to Him
v.10             | Hebrews     | 12      | 5-11               | "The Lord disciplines the one he loves." (ESV) The New Testament's fullest development of Elihu's insight about disciplinary suffering
v.11             | Deuteronomy | 30      | 15-20              | The choice between obedience (life/prosperity) and disobedience (death)
v.15             | Romans      | 5       | 3-4                | "Suffering produces endurance, and endurance produces character, and character produces hope." (ESV)
v.22             | Isaiah      | 55      | 8-9                | "For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways, declares the LORD." (ESV)
v.26             | Psalm       | 90      | 2                  | "From everlasting to everlasting, you are God." (ESV) God is unsearchable in years

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-36/
  Guzik identifies vv.8-15 as Elihu's most significant
  contribution to the book's theology. The concept of suffering
  as DISCIPLINE rather than PUNISHMENT is genuinely different from
  the three friends' retribution theology. Hebrews 12:5-11 will
  later develop this same insight. Guzik notes that while this
  insight is valid, it does not fully explain Job's situation
  either — Job's suffering is not disciplinary (God declared him
  blameless). But it is CLOSER to the truth than the friends'
  position.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'Who teacheth like him?' No schoolmaster compares to God. His
  classroom is the world, His textbook is providence, and His
  most advanced lessons are taught in the darkness of affliction.
  Elihu grasps what the friends missed: suffering can be God's
  pedagogy rather than God's punishment. The difference is
  everything. Punishment looks backward at guilt; discipline looks
  forward to growth. God is forming character, not settling scores.
  'He openeth their ear' — pain makes us listen as nothing else
  can."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.5              | KJV         | "despiseth not any" / ESV: "does not despise any" — Hebrew "lo yimas" = does not reject/despise. God's might does not lead to contempt for humans. He is powerful AND attentive to the small.
v.14             | KJV         | "their life is among the unclean" / ESV: "their life ends among the cult prostitutes" — Hebrew "qedeshim" = cult/temple prostitutes. The ESV makes explicit what the KJV obscures: those who refuse God's discipline die degraded lives among the most debased.
v.16             | KJV         | "out of the strait into a broad place" / ESV: "out of distress into a broad place" — Hebrew "tsarah" = distress/narrowness. "Rachab" = broad/spacious. God's intention was to move Job from constriction to freedom — but Job must cooperate through trust.
v.33             | KJV         | "the cattle also concerning the vapour" / ESV: "the cattle also declare that he rises" — This verse is extremely difficult in Hebrew. The general sense: even animals sense the approaching storm that declares God's power.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Discipline        | Hebrew "musar" = instruction, correction, training. Not punitive destruction but formative education. The purpose is transformation, not retribution. Cf. Proverbs 3:11-12; Hebrews 12:5-11. | 
Fetters/          | Metaphors for suffering and constraint. "Bound in                                 | 
Cords             | fetters" and "holden in cords of affliction" describe being restricted by painful circumstances. Elihu argues these constraints are God's means of getting attention and redirecting the soul. | 
Broad place       | Hebrew "rachab" = spacious, wide, free. The opposite of "strait" (narrow/constricted). God's goal is to bring His people from the narrow place of affliction into the broad place of freedom and blessing. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Suffering can be God's classroom (vv.8-10). Not all suffering is
   punishment. Sometimes it is education. The question in affliction is not
   only "what did I do wrong?" but "what is God teaching?" Pain opens ears
   that prosperity kept closed.

2. God does not despise the small (v.5). His power does not make Him distant
   from human affairs. He is mighty AND attentive, sovereign AND caring. The
   strongest Being in existence is also the most attentive to the weakest.

3. Obedience in suffering determines the outcome (vv.11-12). The same
   affliction that leads one person to growth leads another to destruction —
   the difference is response. Will you listen and learn, or rebel and
   harden? The fire purifies gold and consumes straw.

4. God is the supreme teacher (v.22). His methods include pain, His
   curriculum includes loss, and His classroom includes darkness. But no
   graduate of God's school is unchanged. The lessons of affliction, though
   brutal, produce wisdom that no other instructor can give.

------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Job Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/job/

David Guzik — Job 36 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-36/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

37.notes:
Job - Chapter 37
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Elihu's fourth speech concluded — continues describing God's majestic power in thunderstorms, ice, and weather, then challenges Job: can you do what God does? The Almighty is beyond our reach in power and justice.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown (see Chapter 1 notes for full discussion).

Historical Context: Chapter 37 continues and concludes Elihu's
fourth speech, moving from the theological argument of chapter 36
into a sustained meditation on God's majesty displayed in nature.
This chapter functions as the direct preparation for God's
appearance in chapter 38. As Elihu describes God's power in
thunder, lightning, snow, ice, wind, and storm, the imagery
builds toward a crescendo — and God Himself will soon speak from
that very whirlwind (38:1). Whether intentionally or not, Elihu's
nature hymn serves as the herald's announcement before the King's
arrival. His final words — "we cannot find him out" and "he
respecteth not any that are wise of heart" — prepare perfectly
for God's devastating appearance.

God's Voice in Thunder (vv.1-5): Elihu's heart trembles (v.1) at
God's thunderous voice (v.2). God directs His lightning across
the whole earth (v.3). His voice roars (v.4). "God thundereth
marvellously with his voice; great things doeth he, which we
cannot comprehend" (v.5). This is the key theme: God does what
we CANNOT COMPREHEND. The limit of human understanding is not
God's failure but man's finitude.

God's Power in Weather (vv.6-13): God commands snow to fall (v.6),
seals up human hands in winter (v.7), drives beasts to shelter
(v.8), sends whirlwinds (v.9) and frost (v.10), loads clouds with
moisture (v.11), guides storms by His counsel (v.12). And the
purpose? "Whether for correction, or for his land, or for mercy"
(v.13). Even storms have multiple purposes: discipline, provision,
or love. Not every storm is judgment — some are mercy in disguise.

The Challenge to Job (vv.14-20): "Stand still, and consider the
wondrous works of God" (v.14). Do you know how God commands the
clouds? (v.15). Do you know the "balancings of the clouds" (v.16)?
Can you spread out the sky (v.18)? "Teach us what we shall say
unto him; for we cannot order our speech by reason of darkness"
(v.19). Elihu acknowledges human inability before God — and in
doing so, anticipates God's own interrogation of Job in ch.38-39.

The Unapproachable God (vv.21-24): The conclusion is majestic:
"Fair weather cometh out of the north: with God is terrible
majesty" (v.22). "Touching the Almighty, we cannot find him out:
he is excellent in power, and in judgment, and in plenty of
justice" (v.23). Therefore "men do fear him: he respecteth not
any that are wise of heart" (v.24). Elihu ends with what amounts
to a theological overture: God is coming, and no human wisdom
can prepare you to stand before Him.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.5              | Psalm       | 29      | 3-9                | "The voice of the LORD is over the waters" (ESV) — the great thunder psalm
v.6              | Psalm       | 147     | 16-18              | "He gives snow like wool; he scatters frost like ashes." (ESV)
v.7              | Psalm       | 19      | 1-4                | "The heavens declare the glory of God" — nature reveals God's handiwork
v.13             | Jonah       | 4       | 2                  | God shows mercy even in judgment — multiple purposes coexist in divine action
v.14             | Psalm       | 46      | 10                 | "Be still, and know that I am God." The same call to cease striving and recognize God's sovereignty
v.16             | Job         | 38      | 4-7                | God will soon ask Job exactly these kinds of questions about creation
v.22             | Exodus      | 24      | 17                 | "The appearance of the glory of the LORD was like a devouring fire" (ESV) — God's awesome majesty
v.23             | Isaiah      | 55      | 8-9                | "For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-37/
  Guzik notes that Elihu's nature hymn in chapters 36-37
  functions as a literary and theological bridge to God's
  speeches. The questions Elihu asks ("Do you know? Can you?")
  are exactly the kind of questions God will ask. Guzik observes
  that v.13 is particularly insightful: acknowledging multiple
  purposes for suffering (correction, provision, or mercy) avoids
  the friends' simplistic single-cause explanations.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'Stand still, and consider' — this is the word before God
  speaks. Before the whirlwind, there must be stillness. Before
  revelation, there must be receptivity. Elihu's best moment is
  this command to Job: stop talking and start looking. The God
  who thunders through clouds and freezes waters with His breath
  is the same God who governs your life. If you cannot comprehend
  the storm, how will you comprehend the Stormmaker? And if you
  cannot comprehend Him, perhaps you should trust Him instead."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.7              | KJV         | "He sealeth up the hand of every man" / ESV: "He seals up the hand of every man" — In winter, when snow stops outdoor work, God effectively "seals" human hands — forcing rest and recognition: "that all men may know his work" (KJV). Forced inactivity becomes opportunity for contemplation.
v.9              | KJV         | "Out of the south cometh the whirlwind" / ESV: "From its chamber comes the whirlwind" — Hebrew "cheder" = inner room/chamber. The whirlwind emerges from God's "storeroom." The south/north references may indicate the whirlwind comes from one direction and cold from another.
v.22             | KJV         | "Fair weather cometh out of the north" / ESV: "Out of the north comes golden splendor" — Hebrew "zahab" = gold. The golden light of clearing skies after a storm. The ESV captures the visual image; the KJV interprets it as fair weather. Both suggest the breaking of glory after storm — foreshadowing God's theophany.
v.23             | KJV         | "he will not afflict" / ESV: "he will not violate" — The final phrase is debated. KJV: God will not afflict (unjustly). ESV: God will not violate (justice). Both assert that God's power is governed by His righteousness.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Mazzaroth/        | The constellations or zodiacal stars (mentioned in                                | 
Arcturus          | 38:32 but anticipated here). God guides celestial bodies — another demonstration of His ordering power. | 
Terrible          | Hebrew "nora" = awesome, fearsome, inspiring dread                                | 
majesty           | and wonder. Not "terrible" in the modern sense of "bad" but in the archaic sense of "terrifying in greatness." KJV "terrible majesty" = awe-inspiring grandeur. | 
Balancings        | Hebrew "miphlas" (v.16) = equilibrium, poise. The                                 | 
of clouds      clouds are "balanced" | suspended in air despite carrying enormous weight of water. This is a wonder Job cannot explain, pointing to a Wisdom he cannot fathom. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Stand still before you speak (v.14). Elihu's wisest counsel is his
   simplest: stop, look, consider. Before demanding answers from God,
   consider what He has already shown you. Creation itself is a sermon — are
   you listening?

2. Not every storm is judgment (v.13). Storms come for correction, for
   provision, or for mercy. The same rain that floods can also water. The
   same wind that destroys can also cleanse. Do not assume the purpose of
   your storm — it may be mercy wearing the disguise of hardship.

3. God cannot be fully comprehended (v.23). This is not frustrating — it is
   freeing. If you could fully understand God, He would be no greater than
   your mind. The incomprehensibility of God is the guarantee that He is
   truly God. Trust what exceeds your understanding.

4. Human wisdom does not impress God (v.24). Being "wise of heart" gains no
   special standing before God. He does not respect human cleverness. He
   respects humility, fear, and trust. The wisest response to God's majesty
   is not to explain it but to bow before it.

5. Elihu ends where faith begins: at the limit of knowledge. "We cannot find
   him out" — and yet He can find us. He found Job in chapter 38. He will
   find you in your storm. The God you cannot discover is the God who
   reveals Himself.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Job Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/job/

David Guzik — Job 37 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-37/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

38.ESV:
1. Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind and said:
2. “Who is this that darkens counsel by words without knowledge?
3. Dress for action like a man; I will question you, and you make it known to me.
4. “Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding.
5. Who determined its measurements—surely you know! Or who stretched the line upon it?
6. On what were its bases sunk, or who laid its cornerstone,
7. when the morning stars sang together and all the sons of God shouted for joy?
8. “Or who shut in the sea with doors when it burst out from the womb,
9. when I made clouds its garment and thick darkness its swaddling band,
10. and prescribed limits for it and set bars and doors,
11. and said, ‘Thus far shall you come, and no farther, and here shall your proud waves be stayed’?
12. “Have you commanded the morning since your days began, and caused the dawn to know its place,
13. that it might take hold of the skirts of the earth, and the wicked be shaken out of it?
14. It is changed like clay under the seal, and its features stand out like a garment.
15. From the wicked their light is withheld, and their uplifted arm is broken.
16. “Have you entered into the springs of the sea, or walked in the recesses of the deep?
17. Have the gates of death been revealed to you, or have you seen the gates of deep darkness?
18. Have you comprehended the expanse of the earth? Declare, if you know all this.
19. “Where is the way to the dwelling of light, and where is the place of darkness,
20. that you may take it to its territory and that you may discern the paths to its home?
21. You know, for you were born then, and the number of your days is great!
22. “Have you entered the storehouses of the snow, or have you seen the storehouses of the hail,
23. which I have reserved for the time of trouble, for the day of battle and war?
24. What is the way to the place where the light is distributed, or where the east wind is scattered upon the earth?
25. “Who has cleft a channel for the torrents of rain and a way for the thunderbolt,
26. to bring rain on a land where no man is, on the desert in which there is no man,
27. to satisfy the waste and desolate land, and to make the ground sprout with grass?
28. “Has the rain a father, or who has begotten the drops of dew?
29. From whose womb did the ice come forth, and who has given birth to the frost of heaven?
30. The waters become hard like stone, and the face of the deep is frozen.
31. “Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades or loose the cords of Orion?
32. Can you lead forth the Mazzaroth in their season, or can you guide the Bear with its children?
33. Do you know the ordinances of the heavens? Can you establish their rule on the earth?
34. “Can you lift up your voice to the clouds, that a flood of waters may cover you?
35. Can you send forth lightnings, that they may go and say to you, ‘Here we are’?
36. Who has put wisdom in the inward parts or given understanding to the mind?
37. Who can number the clouds by wisdom? Or who can tilt the waterskins of the heavens,
38. when the dust runs into a mass and the clods stick fast together?
39. “Can you hunt the prey for the lion, or satisfy the appetite of the young lions,
40. when they crouch in their dens or lie in wait in their thicket?
41. Who provides for the raven its prey, when its young ones cry to God for help, and wander about for lack of food?
38.KJV:
1. Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind, and said,
2. Who is this that darkeneth counsel by words without knowledge?
3. Gird up now thy loins like a man; for I will demand of thee, and answer thou me.
4. Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? declare, if thou hast understanding.
5. Who hath laid the measures thereof, if thou knowest? or who hath stretched the line upon it?
6. Whereupon are the foundations thereof fastened? or who laid the corner stone thereof;
7. When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy?
8. Or who shut up the sea with doors, when it brake forth, as if it had issued out of the womb?
9. When I made the cloud the garment thereof, and thick darkness a swaddlingband for it,
10. And brake up for it my decreed place, and set bars and doors,
11. And said, Hitherto shalt thou come, but no further: and here shall thy proud waves be stayed?
12. Hast thou commanded the morning since thy days; and caused the dayspring to know his place;
13. That it might take hold of the ends of the earth, that the wicked might be shaken out of it?
14. It is turned as clay to the seal; and they stand as a garment.
15. And from the wicked their light is withholden, and the high arm shall be broken.
16. Hast thou entered into the springs of the sea? or hast thou walked in the search of the depth?
17. Have the gates of death been opened unto thee? or hast thou seen the doors of the shadow of death?
18. Hast thou perceived the breadth of the earth? declare if thou knowest it all.
19. Where is the way where light dwelleth? and as for darkness, where is the place thereof,
20. That thou shouldest take it to the bound thereof, and that thou shouldest know the paths to the house thereof?
21. Knowest thou it, because thou wast then born? or because the number of thy days is great?

38.notes:
Job - Chapter 38
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God's first speech begins — the LORD answers Job out of the whirlwind, overwhelming him with questions about creation: the earth's foundations, the sea, dawn, snow, stars, and weather — 'Where were you?'

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown (see Chapter 1 notes for full discussion).

Historical Context: Chapter 38 is the climax the entire book has
been building toward: GOD SPEAKS. After 37 chapters of human
dialogue — friends arguing, Job protesting, Elihu intervening —
God Himself breaks into the conversation "out of the whirlwind"
(v.1). This is one of the most powerful theophanies in Scripture.
Yet God's speech is not what anyone expected. He does not explain
Job's suffering. He does not vindicate Job against the friends.
He does not reveal the heavenly council scene from chapters 1-2.
Instead, He interrogates Job with a relentless series of
questions about CREATION. "Where were you?" "Can you?" "Do you
know?" God answers Job's demand for explanation with a demand
for humility. The implicit argument: if you cannot understand how
I made the world, how can you understand how I govern it?

The Opening Challenge (vv.1-3): "Who is this that darkeneth
counsel by words without knowledge?" (v.2). God does not address
Job gently — He confronts him. Job's words have been "without
knowledge" — not sinful (God will vindicate Job in 42:7) but
presumptuous. Job spoke rightly about God's character but wrongly
in demanding that God explain Himself. "Gird up now thy loins
like a man" (v.3) — prepare yourself, because God is about to
ask questions Job cannot answer.

The Foundation of the Earth (vv.4-7): "Where wast thou when I
laid the foundations of the earth?" (v.4). The first question
establishes the fundamental disproportion: God was creating while
Job did not yet exist. The morning stars sang at creation (v.7),
the sons of God shouted for joy — but Job was not there. He
cannot explain the beginning; how can he explain the middle?

The Sea (vv.8-11): God "shut up the sea with doors" (v.8), made
clouds its garment (v.9), and decreed: "Hitherto shalt thou
come, but no further" (v.11). God controls the most powerful,
chaotic force in nature. If He can set boundaries for the ocean,
can He not set boundaries for suffering?

Dawn (vv.12-15): Has Job ever commanded morning or caused dawn to
know its place? Can he shake the wicked out of the earth? Dawn
is God's daily miracle that Job takes for granted.

The Deep, Death, Light, and Darkness (vv.16-21): Has Job entered
the springs of the sea, seen the gates of death, comprehended the
earth's expanse, or found where light and darkness dwell? God's
sarcasm in v.21 is sharp: "Knowest thou it, because thou wast
then born? or because the number of thy days is great?" — You
must know all this, since you're so old and wise!

Snow, Hail, Rain, and Ice (vv.22-30): Has Job entered God's
storehouses of snow and hail? (v.22). Does he know how light is
distributed or wind scattered? (v.24). Who sends rain on empty
deserts? (v.26). Who fathers the dew, births the ice? (vv.28-29).
Each question reveals God's sovereign control over phenomena Job
cannot even explain, much less replicate.

Stars and Weather (vv.31-38): Can Job bind the Pleiades, loose
Orion, bring forth Mazzaroth, or guide Arcturus? (vv.31-32).
Does he know heaven's ordinances? (v.33). Can he command clouds
and lightning? (vv.34-35). Who put wisdom in the inward parts?
(v.36). The questions multiply, overwhelming Job's presumption.

Animals (vv.39-41): Can Job hunt for the lion or provide for the
raven? God feeds creatures Job never sees. His providence
operates continuously in places Job will never go.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Exodus      | 19      | 16-19              | God appears at Sinai in thunder, smoke, and fire. God reveals Himself in power
v.1              | 1 Kings     | 19      | 11-12              | God appeared to Elijah after wind, fire, and earthquake. Here He speaks FROM the whirlwind
v.4              | Proverbs    | 8       | 22-30              | Wisdom was present when God laid earth's foundations — but man was not
v.7              | Job         | 1       | 6                  | ; 2:1 — "Sons of God" = the same heavenly beings from the prologue, present at both creation and the heavenly council
v.11             | Psalm       | 104     | 9                  | "You set a boundary that they may not pass, so that they might not again cover the earth." (ESV)
v.22             | Joshua      | 10      | 11                 | God used hailstones as weapons against Israel's enemies — His "storehouses" deployed in battle
v.28             | James       | 1       | 17                 | "Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above" (ESV) — God fathers even the dew
v.31             | Amos        | 5       | 8                  | "He who made the Pleiades and Orion." (ESV) God governs the constellations

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-38/
  Guzik emphasizes that God never answers Job's "why." Instead,
  He overwhelms Job with "who" and "how" — who made this? who
  governs that? The unstated argument: the God who designed and
  sustains all creation can be trusted to govern one man's life
  wisely. Guzik notes that God's questions are not cruel but
  corrective — they reframe Job's perspective from demanding
  answers to receiving God's presence.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "God answers Job — but not with answers. He answers with
  questions. And the questions are not interrogation but
  revelation. Every question reveals something Job did not know,
  could not do, and cannot control. And the cumulative effect is
  not humiliation but worship. For if God governs the stars and
  the sea, the dawn and the deep, the snow and the raven — then
  He surely governs the life of one suffering man. 'Where wast
  thou?' — I was not there. I do not understand. But He was there.
  He does understand. And that is enough."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | ESV: "the whirlwind" — Hebrew "se'arah" = storm wind, tempest. God appears in power and motion, not stillness. The whirlwind was prepared by Elihu's storm descriptions in chapters 36-37.
v.3              | KJV         | "Gird up now thy loins like a man" / ESV: "Dress for action like a man" — The ESV modernizes the idiom. The image: tuck your robe into your belt to prepare for strenuous activity. God challenges Job to stand up and face the questions.
v.7              | KJV         | "morning stars" / ESV: "morning stars" — Likely angelic beings (paralleled with "sons of God" in the same verse). Stars and angels are associated in ancient Hebrew thought (cf. Judges 5:20; Revelation 1:20).
v.31             | KJV         | "sweet influences of Pleiades" / ESV: "chains of the Pleiades" — Hebrew "ma'adannot" is debated: bonds, chains, or influences. The Pleiades are a tight star cluster; God "binds" them together. Can Job do the same?
v.32             | KJV         | "Mazzaroth" / ESV: "Mazzaroth" — Likely the zodiacal constellations or a specific constellation. The identity is uncertain, but the point is clear: God guides celestial bodies that humans can only observe.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Whirlwind         | Hebrew "se'arah" — a storm wind, tempest. God's mode of appearance. Connotes power, awe, and the overwhelming presence of the divine. Also used in 2 Kings 2:1 when Elijah was taken to heaven. | 
Pleiades          | A cluster of stars in the constellation Taurus. In ancient astronomy, their appearance marked planting season. God "binds" them — holds them together in their cluster. | 
Orion             | A prominent constellation. In Hebrew thought, the "bands" of Orion refer to the forces that hold the constellation in its pattern. Only God can "loose" them. | 
Mazzaroth         | Uncertain identification. Possibly the zodiacal constellations that mark the seasons, or a specific constellation. The point: God brings them forth in their appointed time, governing celestial rhythms. | 
Cornerstone       | v.6: The cornerstone of the earth — creation language describing God as master architect who measured, planned, and built the world with precision. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's answer to suffering is not explanation but presence (v.1). Job
   asked "why?" God answered "I AM." The theophany itself is the answer —
   not information about suffering but encounter with the Sufferer's God.
   Sometimes God's presence IS the answer, even without words.

2. You were not there (v.4). This is humbling and freeing. You were not
   consulted at creation; you will not be consulted about providence. God
   does not need your input to be just. Your inability to understand is not
   a flaw — it is a feature of being finite before the Infinite.

3. God governs chaos (v.11). The sea obeys. The deep has boundaries.
   Darkness has limits. Whatever feels chaotic and overwhelming in your life
   is still within God's decreed boundaries. "Hitherto shalt thou come, but
   no further."

4. Creation reveals enough of God to trust Him (entire chapter). Every
   question God asks points to a truth Job can observe: God designed,
   sustains, and governs all things. If you can trust Him with the cosmos,
   you can trust Him with your crisis.

5. Questions can be more powerful than answers. God teaches Job through
   interrogation, not lecture. Each question forces Job to encounter his own
   limits — and in encountering his limits, to encounter God's
   limitlessness. Sometimes the best pastoral response is not an answer but
   a better question.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Job Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/job/

David Guzik — Job 38 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-38/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

39.ESV:
1. “Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Do you observe the calving of the does?
2. Can you number the months that they fulfill, and do you know the time when they give birth,
3. when they crouch, bring forth their offspring, and are delivered of their young?
4. Their young ones become strong; they grow up in the open; they go out and do not return to them.
5. “Who has let the wild donkey go free? Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey,
6. to whom I have given the arid plain for his home and the salt land for his dwelling place?
7. He scorns the tumult of the city; he hears not the shouts of the driver.
8. He ranges the mountains as his pasture, and he searches after every green thing.
9. “Is the wild ox willing to serve you? Will he spend the night at your manger?
10. Can you bind him in the furrow with ropes, or will he harrow the valleys after you?
11. Will you depend on him because his strength is great, and will you leave to him your labor?
12. Do you have faith in him that he will return your grain and gather it to your threshing floor?
13. “The wings of the ostrich wave proudly, but are they the pinions and plumage of love?
14. For she leaves her eggs to the earth and lets them be warmed on the ground,
15. forgetting that a foot may crush them and that the wild beast may trample them.
16. She deals cruelly with her young, as if they were not hers; though her labor be in vain, yet she has no fear,
17. because God has made her forget wisdom and given her no share in understanding.
18. When she rouses herself to flee, she laughs at the horse and his rider.
19. “Do you give the horse his might? Do you clothe his neck with a mane?
20. Do you make him leap like the locust? His majestic snorting is terrifying.
21. He paws in the valley and exults in his strength; he goes out to meet the weapons.
22. He laughs at fear and is not dismayed; he does not turn back from the sword.
23. Upon him rattle the quiver, the flashing spear, and the javelin.
24. With fierceness and rage he swallows the ground; he cannot stand still at the sound of the trumpet.
25. When the trumpet sounds, he says ‘Aha!’ He smells the battle from afar, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
26. “Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars and spreads his wings toward the south?
27. Is it at your command that the eagle mounts up and makes his nest on high?
28. On the rock he dwells and makes his home, on the rocky crag and stronghold.
29. From there he spies out the prey; his eyes behold it from far away.
30. His young ones suck up blood, and where the slain are, there is he.”
39.KJV:
1. Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth? or canst thou mark when the hinds do calve?
2. Canst thou number the months that they fulfil? or knowest thou the time when they bring forth?
3. They bow themselves, they bring forth their young ones, they cast out their sorrows.
4. Their young ones are in good liking, they grow up with corn; they go forth, and return not unto them.
5. Who hath sent out the wild ass free? or who hath loosed the bands of the wild ass?
6. Whose house I have made the wilderness, and the barren land his dwellings.
7. He scorneth the multitude of the city, neither regardeth he the crying of the driver.
8. The range of the mountains is his pasture, and he searcheth after every green thing.
9. Will the unicorn be willing to serve thee, or abide by thy crib?
10. Canst thou bind the unicorn with his band in the furrow? or will he harrow the valleys after thee?
11. Wilt thou trust him, because his strength is great? or wilt thou leave thy labour to him?
12. Wilt thou believe him, that he will bring home thy seed, and gather it into thy barn?
13. Gavest thou the goodly wings unto the peacocks? or wings and feathers unto the ostrich?
14. Which leaveth her eggs in the earth, and warmeth them in dust,
15. And forgetteth that the foot may crush them, or that the wild beast may break them.
16. She is hardened against her young ones, as though they were not hers: her labour is in vain without fear;
17. Because God hath deprived her of wisdom, neither hath he imparted to her understanding.
18. What time she lifteth up herself on high, she scorneth the horse and his rider.
19. Hath thou given the horse strength? hast thou clothed his neck with thunder?
20. Canst thou make him afraid as a grasshopper? the glory of his nostrils is terrible.
21. He paweth in the valley, and rejoiceth in his strength: he goeth on to meet the armed men.
22. He mocketh at fear, and is not affrighted; neither turneth he back from the sword.
23. The quiver rattleth against him, the glittering spear and the shield.
24. He swalloweth the ground with fierceness and rage: neither believeth he that it is the sound of the trumpet.
25. He saith among the trumpets, Ha, ha; and he smelleth the battle afar off, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
26. Doth the hawk fly by thy wisdom, and stretch her wings toward the south?
27. Doth the eagle mount up at thy command, and make her nest on high?
28. She dwelleth and abideth on the rock, upon the crag of the rock, and the strong place.
29. From thence she seeketh the prey, and her eyes behold afar off.
30. Her young ones also suck up blood: and where the slain are, there is she.
39.ASV:
1. Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth? [Or] canst thou mark when the hinds do calve?
2. Canst thou number the months that they fulfil? Or knowest thou the time when they bring forth?
3. They bow themselves, they bring forth their young, They cast out their pains.
4. Their young ones become strong, they grow up in the open field; They go forth, and return not again.
5. Who hath sent out the wild ass free? Or who hath loosed the bonds of the swift ass,
6. Whose home I have made the wilderness, And the salt land his dwelling-place?
7. He scorneth the tumult of the city, Neither heareth he the shoutings of the driver.
8. The range of the mountains is his pasture, And he searcheth after every green thing.
9. Will the wild-ox be content to serve thee? Or will he abide by thy crib?
10. Canst thou bind the wild-ox with his band in the furrow? Or will he harrow the valleys after thee?
11. Wilt thou trust him, because his strength is great? Or wilt thou leave to him thy labor?
12. Wilt thou confide in him, that he will bring home thy seed, And gather [the grain] of thy threshing-floor?
13. The wings of the ostrich wave proudly; [But] are they the pinions and plumage of love?
14. For she leaveth her eggs on the earth, And warmeth them in the dust,
15. And forgetteth that the foot may crush them, Or that the wild beast may trample them.
16. She dealeth hardly with her young ones, as if they were not hers: Though her labor be in vain, [she is] without fear;
17. Because God hath deprived her of wisdom, Neither hath he imparted to her understanding.
18. What time she lifteth up herself on high, She scorneth the horse and his rider.
19. Hast thou given the horse [his] might? Hast thou clothed his neck with the quivering mane?
20. Hast thou made him to leap as a locust? The glory of his snorting is terrible.
21. He paweth in the valley, and rejoiceth in his strength: He goeth out to meet the armed men.
22. He mocketh at fear, and is not dismayed; Neither turneth he back from the sword.
23. The quiver rattleth against him, The flashing spear and the javelin.
24. He swalloweth the ground with fierceness and rage; Neither believeth he that it is the voice of the trumpet.
25. As oft as the trumpet [soundeth] he saith, Aha! And he smelleth the battle afar off, The thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
26. Is it by thy wisdom that the hawk soareth, (And) stretcheth her wings toward the south?
27. Is it at thy command that the eagle mounteth up, And maketh her nest on high?
28. On the cliff she dwelleth, and maketh her home, Upon the point of the cliff, and the stronghold.
29. From thence she spieth out the prey; Her eyes behold it afar off.
30. Her young ones also suck up blood: And where the slain are, there is she.
39.NET:

2. Do you count the months they must fulfill, and do you know the time they give birth?

39.notes:
Job - Chapter 39
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God's first speech continued — questions Job about the wild animals: mountain goats, wild donkeys, the wild ox, the ostrich, the war horse, the hawk, and the eagle — creatures beyond human control.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown (see Chapter 1 notes for full discussion).

Historical Context: Chapter 39 continues God's first speech from
the whirlwind, shifting from cosmic phenomena (ch.38: earth, sea,
dawn, snow, stars, weather) to the animal kingdom. God surveys
creatures that live entirely outside human control: mountain
goats, wild donkeys, wild oxen, ostriches, horses, hawks, and
eagles. The theological point is consistent: God's wisdom and
care extend to realms Job never touches. Creatures Job cannot
tame, feed, or even observe are sustained by God's providence
daily. If God's wisdom operates in the wild places beyond human
reach, it also operates in the painful places beyond human
understanding.

Mountain Goats and Deer (vv.1-4): Do you know when they give
birth? Can you number their months? God oversees the reproductive
cycles of animals in remote places where no human watches. His
care does not require human awareness to function.

The Wild Donkey (vv.5-8): God set the wild donkey free (v.5) —
gave it the desert for a home (v.6). It scorns the city and
ignores the driver's shouts (v.7). God created creatures that
deliberately resist human domestication. Freedom and wildness are
part of His design, not flaws in it.

The Wild Ox (vv.9-12): "Will the unicorn be willing to serve
thee?" (KJV v.9). The wild ox is powerful but untameable. Job
cannot harness it, trust it, or make it work his fields. God
created strength that serves no human purpose — because not
everything exists FOR humans.

The Ostrich (vv.13-18): The most unusual passage. God describes
the ostrich as foolish: she leaves her eggs unprotected (v.14),
deals cruelly with her young (v.16), and lacks wisdom (v.17) —
"because God hath deprived her of wisdom" (v.17). Yet she can
outrun a horse (v.18). God made a creature that is simultaneously
foolish and magnificent. Not all of God's creation fits neat
categories of "wise" or "useful." Some creatures are simply
wild and strange — and God delights in them.

The War Horse (vv.19-25): One of the most magnificent animal
descriptions in all literature. "Hast thou given the horse
strength?" (v.19). The horse laughs at fear (v.22), does not
turn from the sword (v.22), paws the ground with fierceness
(v.24), and says "Ha, ha!" among the trumpets (v.25). He smells
battle from afar. God made a creature of terrible beauty — fierce,
fearless, and exhilarating. This is God's artistry on display.

Hawk and Eagle (vv.26-30): "Doth the hawk fly by thy wisdom?"
(v.26). Does the eagle mount up at Job's command? (v.27). These
birds of prey operate by God's instinct, not human instruction.
They nest on unreachable crags (v.28), see prey from impossible
distances (v.29). God governs the skies without human help.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 104     | 18                 | "The high mountains are for the wild goats" (ESV) — God provides habitat for creatures humans rarely see
v.5              | Genesis     | 16      | 12                 | Ishmael described as a "wild ass of a man" — freedom and wildness associated with desert life
v.9              | ?           | ?       | ?                  | KJV "unicorn" — Numbers 23:22 — "God brings them out of Egypt and is for them like the horns of the wild ox." (ESV) Same Hebrew word (re'em = wild ox/aurochs).
v.13             | Lamentations | 4       | 3                  | The ostrich described as cruel to her young — a proverbial image in ancient Israel
v.19             | Revelation  | 19      | 11                 | Christ returns on a white horse — the war horse as symbol of divine power
v.27             | Isaiah      | 40      | 31                 | "They shall mount up with wings like eagles" (ESV) — the eagle as symbol of God-given strength
v.30             | Matthew     | 24      | 28                 | "Wherever the corpse is, there the vultures will gather." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-39/
  Guzik emphasizes that God's animal survey makes a single point
  repeatedly: God's wisdom, care, and sovereign design extend to
  realms entirely beyond human control or understanding. If God
  governs wild goats, donkeys, oxen, ostriches, horses, hawks,
  and eagles — all without human help — He certainly governs
  human lives without needing to explain Himself. Guzik notes
  the ostrich passage is particularly striking: God made something
  deliberately "foolish" and delights in it. Not all of creation
  is "useful" — some of it is simply wild and wonderful.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "God parades His creatures before Job like an artist displaying
  his gallery. Each one is a masterwork Job did not design, cannot
  control, and barely understands. The wild donkey laughs at the
  city. The ostrich outstrips the horse despite her foolishness.
  The war horse charges into death without flinching. The eagle
  sees what no eye can see from heights no man can reach. And God
  says: I made all of this. Without you. Do you understand now
  why I do not need to explain Myself? I govern more than you
  can imagine, in ways you cannot fathom, for purposes you
  cannot see."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.9              | KJV         | "unicorn" / ESV: "wild ox" — Hebrew "re'em" = a large, wild bovine, likely the now-extinct aurochs. The KJV "unicorn" follows the Septuagint (Greek "monoceros") and does not mean the mythical single-horned horse. The ESV correctly identifies it as a wild ox — powerful and untameable.
v.13             | KJV         | "peacocks... ostrich" / ESV: "ostrich" — The KJV translation of v.13a is uncertain. The Hebrew likely refers to the ostrich's wing feathers throughout. Modern translations consistently render this as the ostrich.
v.19             | KJV         | "clothed his neck with thunder" / ESV: "clothe his neck with a mane" — Hebrew "ra'mah" could be thunder/ shaking or mane/flowing hair. The KJV reading is more dramatic; the ESV more literal. The mane of a galloping horse does create a thundering visual impression.
v.25             | KJV         | "Ha, ha" / ESV: "Aha!" — Hebrew "heach" = an exclamation of excitement or triumph. The horse senses battle and responds with eagerness, not fear. Extraordinary anthropomorphic language for an animal.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Wild ox/          | Hebrew "re'em" — the aurochs (Bos primigenius), an                                | 
Unicorn           | extinct wild bovine that stood up to 6 feet tall. Enormously powerful and completely untameable. The KJV "unicorn" derives from the Greek translation, not from the mythical creature. | 
Ostrich           | Hebrew "rananah" (joyful one — ironic name for a supposedly foolish bird). Known in the ancient world for abandoning eggs and running with astonishing speed. God claims responsibility for both her foolishness and her speed. | 
War horse         | The trained cavalry horse of the ancient Near East. Described with extraordinary vigor: fearless, eager for battle, snorting, pawing, charging. God made it this way — its courage is divine craftsmanship. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Not everything exists to serve you (vv.5-8). The wild donkey scorns human
   use. The wild ox refuses domestication. God made creatures whose entire
   purpose is to be wild and free — not to benefit humans. This challenges
   anthropocentrism: the world is God's theater, not yours. Your
   understanding is not the measure of God's design.

2. God delights in the strange and foolish (v.17). The ostrich is absurd by
   human standards — foolish, cruel to young, yet magnificent in speed. God
   made her and delights in her. Not everything God does must make sense to
   you. Some of His works are simply wild, strange, and glorious — existing
   for His own pleasure, not your comprehension.

3. God gives strength for the task He assigns (v.19). The horse's
   fearlessness is God's gift. The eagle's sight is God's gift. The hawk's
   flight is God's gift. Whatever task God assigns you, He provides the
   capacity for it. You do not need to generate strength — you receive it.

4. God governs what you cannot even observe (vv.1-4, 26-30). Mountain goats
   give birth in places you'll never see. Eagles nest on crags you'll never
   reach. God's providence operates continuously in the unseen. If He
   governs what you cannot observe, He certainly governs what you cannot
   understand.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Job Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/job/

David Guzik — Job 39 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/job-39/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)
